claims.claim: The SIB exhibited a thermal runaway trigger temperature of 58.15°C, while the LFP battery triggered thermal runaway at 196.65°C, demonstrating significantly lower thermal stability in the SIB.
claims.quote: The LFP battery reached TR at approximately 1659 s with a measured temperature of 208.13 °C, while the SIB exhibited earlier TR onset at 530 s with a notably lower surface temperature of 58.15 °C. The Ttr for LFP significantly exceeds that of SIB (LFP: 208.13 °C vs SIB: 58.15 °C), demonstrating superior thermal stability in the LFP system.

claims.claim: The time interval between safety valve opening and thermal runaway (ΔtSV-TR) was 12 s for SIB and 173 s for LFP, indicating significantly shorter emergency response time available for SIB.
claims.quote: The ΔtSV-TR was 12 s for SIB and 173 s for LFP. A comparison of these two values indicates that, under the same heating power conditions, the time available for fire prevention and evacuation is significantly shorter for SIB, thereby increasing the risks associated with TR.

claims.claim: The SIB's explosive limit range (ELR) after including EMC contribution was 32.5%, while the LFP's ELR was 21.21%, indicating greater combustion and explosion risk for SIB.
claims.quote: After considering the contribution of EMC, the explosive limit range of the SIB was reduced to 4.96 %–37.46 % with an ELR of 32.5 %, while that of the LFP battery decreased to 4.11 %–25.32 % with an ELR of 21.21 %.

claims.claim: The maximum mass loss rate per unit energy of SIB (0.33 g s⁻¹ Wh⁻¹) was approximately 6.6 times higher than that of LFP (0.05 g s⁻¹ Wh⁻¹) during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The maximum mass loss rate of SIB reached 172.6 g s−1, normalized to 0.33 g s−1 Wh−1, while that of LFP was only 25.6 g s−1, normalized to 0.05 g s−1 Wh−1.

claims.claim: The SIB produced approximately four times more total gas per unit energy than the LFP during thermal runaway (0.72 L Wh⁻¹ vs 0.18 L Wh⁻¹).
claims.quote: the total gas production is 0.72 L Wh−1 and 0.18 L Wh−1 for the SIB and LFP, respectively, indicating that the SIB produces approximately four times more gas than the LFP.

claims.claim: NMC cells exhibited earlier thermal runaway onset (173.6–215.1 °C), with higher-SOC cells initiating runaway at lower temperatures, whereas LFP cells required higher onset temperatures (241.9–249.2 °C) to trigger TR.
claims.quote: NMC cells exhibited earlier TR onset (173.6–215.1 ◦C), with higher-SOC cells initiating runaway at lower temperatures, whereas LFP cells required higher onset temperatures (241.9–249.2 ◦C) to trigger TR, reflecting greater thermal stability.

claims.claim: NMC cells at 100% SOC lost 57.7–61.4% of their mass during thermal runaway, while LFP cells showed consistently lower mass loss of approximately 11–13% with little variation between SOC levels.
claims.quote: NMC cells exhibited substantially higher mass loss, with 57.7–61.4 % of mass lost at 100 % SOC. In contrast, LFP cells showed consistently lower mass loss of ~11–13 %, with little variation between SOC levels.

claims.claim: Peak HF emissions exceeded 66,000 ppm during thermal runaway, as measured in the near-field vent plume of 18650 LFP cells at 100% SOC.

HF first appeared immediately after safety venting onset, providing a finite early-warning interval before thermal runaway; the venting window was approximately 1 minute for NMC and approximately 5 minutes for LFP under ~27 °C/min external heating.
claims.quote: HF becomes detectable immediately after safety venting, providing a finite warning interval before it decays and well before TR onset. In our tests, the venting window was ≈1 min for NMC and ≈5 min for LFP (Table 2), offering practical time for early detection, mitigation, and response.

claims.claim: Peak HF in LFP cells displayed a strong SOC dependence at both venting and TR stages, with the 50% SOC case producing approximately half the peak HF of the 100% SOC case.
claims.quote: peak HF in LFP displayed a strong SOC dependence at both venting and TR, with the 50 % SOC case approximately half of the 100 % SOC case.

claims.claim: Conventional gas analysis techniques using unheated or metallic transfer lines often underestimate or entirely lose HF due to its highly adsorptive and corrosive nature, causing existing safety models to be based on HF levels orders of magnitude lower than true concentrations.
claims.quote: Due to its highly adsorptive and corrosive nature, HF is often underestimated, or entirely lost, in unheated or metallic transfer lines. As a result, existing safety models and hazard assessments may be based on HF levels that are orders of magnitude lower than the true concentrations.

claims.claim: LIBs with lithium-nickel-cobalt-aluminium (NCA) cathode chemistry had a laminar burning velocity range between 0.2 m/s to 1.1 m/s, which is higher than typical hydrocarbons.
claims.quote: LIBs with lithium-nickel-cobalt-aluminium (NCA) cathode chemistry had a higher LBV than typical hydrocarbons, with an LBV range between 0.2 m/s to 1.1 m/s.

claims.claim: DNV reported an explosion pressure between 90 kPa (gauge) and 270 kPa (gauge) in a partly confined geometry with various Li-ion vent gas release profiles.
claims.quote: DNV reported an explosion pressure between 90 kPa (gauge) and 270 kPa (gauge) in a partly confined geometry, with various Li-ion vent gas release profiles.

claims.claim: The maximum explosion pressure in the 1-m channel experiments was 1.36 kPa (gauge) at equivalence ratio of 1.08, while the CFD simulation predicted 1.45 kPa (gauge) at equivalence ratio of 1.0.
claims.quote: the maximum explosion pressure was 1.36 kPa (gauge), at φ equal to 1.08; whereas, the maximum explosion pressure for the simulation was 1.45 kPa (gauge), at φ equal to 1.0.

claims.claim: At 650 K, the Gülder equation underpredicts LBVs on the lean and rich side with a standard deviation of error of 338 mm/s and R² of 0.66.
claims.quote: At 650 K, the SDE is 338 mm/s, and R2 is 0.66.

claims.claim: Pressure and flame front velocity oscillations that occurred as the flame approached the channel opening were not accurately predicted by the CFD simulations.
claims.quote: pressure and flame front velocity oscillations, which occurred as the flame approached the channel opening, were not accurately predicted.
